author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody: There is nothing Caribbean about this recipe except the name and use of coconut milk and hot peppers

author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody: I know both salt mackerel and shrimp rundown. Love them both. I've had them with rice, had them with pasta. But this recipe seems strange. It leaves out usual ingredients, adds odd substitutions, and apparently completely overcooks the starch. Rundown on pasta is delicious. I won't try this, however, because of the cooking times, even though it might be delicious. I'll stick with preparing the rundown more traditionally and serving it over pasta. Still, it's great to see rundown profiled!
readable_ai_guess: Thought the cooking times were wrong

author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody: This seems very odd.  Seafood and lemongrass are mentioned among the primary elements, but there is no lemongrass.  I guess the anchovies are the seafood.  And you cook the pasta on medium-high for 10-12 minutes past al dente!  That's an awfully long time.
readable_ai_guess: Thought the cooking times were wrong

author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody: Where is everyone seeing lemongrass in the recipe? I don’t see it.

author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody: They must have edited the recipe and description to remove any reference to lemongrass, unless I'm blind. I don't see it anywhere but in the comments.

author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody: This sounded weird in theory, but it was phenomenal. The flavors all complemented each other, nothing was overpowering. I went light on the habanero for my kids and we all loved it. The sauce is creamy, the flavors are bright, and since it’s all rough chopping, it’s a super easy weeknight meal. I’m not ashamed to say I threw some chicken nuggets in the toaster oven and we had them on the side for some protein on a busy school night. Bonus: it’s made the kids’ lists of approved leftovers to send for school lunches.

author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody: I was apprehensive to attempt this recipe, however knowing that it was authored by Yewande Komolate, it seemed a reasonably low risk. I was right! It is delicious. It definitely contains an odd assortment of ingredients, not usual for a Western palate. In spite of this, or more likely because of it, the dish was very tasty. I did cook the pasta separately and added al dente to the sauce, then cooked it all for another 5 minutes. This will definitely make it into our regular rotation. Yummy!!!

author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody:
Not really Caribbean unless it’s a mixup of various islands plus SE Asia.  To the best of my knowledge, we don’t use lemongrass, at least not in Jamaica, not even sure if most of us from the Caribbean know what anchovies are.  
My mom’s recipe is tomatoes, coconut milk, onions, hot pepper, garlic, thyme if wanted.  Cook until sauce separates, it will look curdled.  Add seafood at the end. We can also add tropical root vegetables instead of the pasta.

author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody: I found this surprisingly tasty.  I did cook the pasta separately (8 oz for 2 people) as I was worried about overcooking it . I also added a head of broccoli cut into florets towards the end and took both off the heat when al dente. I used 1/3 of the anchovies and cooked the tomatoes a bit before adding half the coconut milk and reducing until thick and adding the pasta.  I did not add cheese as that seemed strange. The broccoli made it and the addition of shrimp would be great as well!

author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody: Apart from the lemongrass and no-seafood-but-anchovies issues, this seems like a fascinating recipe. Sort of terrified by the prospect of adding parmesan to a seafood dish though. I'll probably make this with salmon tonight and throw some lemongrass stalks in the sauce when the pasta gets added.

author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody: I put in 1/2 of the anchovies and it was still to much for us.  The next day I tried the leftovers and the anchovy flavor was overbearing.  My wife likes anchovies and she couldn't even do it.    Also, I did think that it was to starchy after cooking the pasta in the sauce.  Overall, I'd probably not make this one again.  It was an easy cook though, so maybe some personal tweaks would make fun dish.
readable_ai_guess: Didn't like recipe due to flavor

author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody: Those who are complaining about the pasta cooking time without having made this (or similar recipes where the pasta cooks in the sauce): Use the recommended pasta shapes, which are sturdier, and follow the cooking times/temperatures, and the pasta and sauce come out with a great consistency. I cannot speak to the authenticity of the recipe, but the cooking technique works fine. The only thing I will say for recipes where the pasta cooks in the sauce, such as this one, is that the pasta tends to start sticking to the bottom of the pot towards the end, so stir and keep an eye on that.

author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody:
I love Yewande.  She understands coconut milk in a spiritual way.  Reading her recipes makes me want to hang out with her. This dish satisfies a desire for tomato sauce that cheers up this insane cold weather in a fresh but also warming way.
Naysayers- you just like complaining.
I never have everything exactly on hand and she still leads me to  favorites that stay on rotation. 
This is now on the playlist.
 Jarred minced ginger and some fish sauce (to my taste-no anchovies) were my only substitutions. So yummy and easy. 

Even the picky eaters were happy.  For protein we paired with a jar of jalapeño oil tonnino and served a yogurty citrus salad on the side.

author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody: I was looking forward to making this dish based on the recipe description however we found it to be quite underwhelming and meh. Followed the recipe as written (used a habanero vs a scotch bonnet pepper) and didn’t think it had much flavor at all. The coconut milk overwhelmed the elements that should have provided more depth of flavor. We think the recipe has possibilities with revisions but felt it was a lot work for little return on the effort.
readable_ai_guess: Didn't like recipe due to flavor

author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody:
This was bad and not in a good way.

The anchovies rendered it really bitter. And the flavors never came together. I don't know what I would do to improve it except not make it.

Family members stopped eating after a couple of bites and we scrambled to put together another makeshift dinner.
readable_ai_guess: Didn't like recipe due to flavor

author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody: I share the confusion raised in many of the comments about the presence/absence of lemongrass, but I'm more puzzled by the thought of making the sauce with the 4 cups of water plus the can of coconut milk. That sounds like a lot of runny sauce to me, not at all like the illustration.
readable_ai_guess: Didn't like recipe due to technique or equipment

Cooked the pasta 10 mins ("cooks in 14") then just a few minutes in the sauce and it was falling apart. The people who said the anchovies were overwhelming...we had the opposite issuse. 8 anchovies and no flavor.

author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody: I added lemongrass and made it with pasta (cooked seperately) it was ok. I added shrimp to the leftover sauce and served it with rice the next day...much better!

author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody: I cooked the pasta separately (by accident!) and added some of the pasta water to the coconut milk  mixture. My husband hates anchovies so skipped those. Did add some arctic char (what we had in the freezer) for a delightful fish stew. Lime was good enough, but I did wonder about the lemongrass...

author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody: I remember many years ago being served a dish on the Atlantic coast of Nicaragua that was called rondon. It took me a while to realize the name came from "rundown". It was a stew with root vegetables and fish, and a coconut milk sauce that would "run down". Very different from what is being proposed here. But this dish does sound good. My impulse would be to cook the pasta separately.

commentBody: Crazy pasta technique - better the next day.

author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody: Made this, it wasn’t quite what I was hoping for as I was tasting so I modified…ok, added cream and so so so much parm. Next time would make with stock instead of water. My picky 8 year old loved it though. Go figure.

author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody: my first comment on Cooking -- this recipe was DELICIOUS.  I'm vegetarian, subbed out anchovies for capers.  And controlled the heat using jalapenos, and then in the when checking seasoning, I stirred some sambal olek.  It was a perfect heat. But i do want to try it with scotch bonnet next time for funsies.   Next time, I'll also try to add protein into it -- maybe garbanzos, or blend some soft tofu into the sauce.

author: Yewande Komolafe
commentBody: Followed the recipe very closely. The results were edible but I didn’t think the recipe has a focus. What is the central flavor being celebrated? I have not spent much time on the Caribbean islands but I wonder if dried rigatoni, Pecorino Romano cheese, tomato paste and anchovies would be widely available for use by local cooks.
